ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Beef soup) is the closest parent. No specific Tom Yam Beef entry exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one bowl (400 g) of Tom Yam Beef soup, typically containing broth, sliced beef, mushrooms, tomatoes, and aromatic herbs.

nutrient_source.title: Tom Yum Soup with Beef — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian spiced beef soups
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Tom Yam Beef entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for spiced beef broth soups (beef, lemongrass, chilli, fish sauce, mushrooms, tomatoes) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data for comparable Southeast Asian beef soups. Per-100g values reflect a typical restaurant-style preparation with moderate broth-to-solid ratio.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed beef and vegetable soups — analogous estimate based on spiced broth-based meat soups; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tom Yam Beef. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous spiced beef and vegetable broth soups from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The low carbohydrate content (3.5 g/100g), high protein, and acidic lime/fish sauce components are consistent with a low GI response. Glycemic load per serving = (45 × 3.5 × 400) / (100 × 100) = 6.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and broth
hypertension.guidance: Tom Yam Beef contains approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1680 mg per standard 400 g bowl), which represents a substantial portion of the 2000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced fish sauce and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tom Yam Beef has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 45 and only 3.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 6.3 per standard serving, making it a suitable choice for individuals managing blood glucose when consumed without added noodles or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium from fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tom Yam Beef provides approximately 1.1 g saturated fat and 420 mg sodium per 100 g; while the saturated fat level is moderate and the dish contains beneficial anti-inflammatory aromatics such as lemongrass and galangal, the high sodium content warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease who should limit total sodium intake to under 1500–2000 mg per day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tom Yam Beef (ต้มยำเนื้อ) is a Thai spiced hot-and-sour beef soup made by simmering sliced beef in an aromatic broth flavoured with lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, galangal, fresh chilli, fish sauce, and lime juice, typically garnished with mushrooms, tomatoes, and spring onions. The dish originates from Thailand and is a beef variant of the classic Tom Yam (Tom Yum) soup family, which is one of the most internationally recognised Thai culinary preparations. In Singapore, it is served at Thai restaurants, hawker stalls, and casual dining establishments, sometimes accompanied by steamed rice or noodles. The flavour profile is intensely sour, spicy, and savoury with a fragrant herbal aroma from the lemongrass and kaffir lime. The broth is thin and clear to slightly cloudy. Nutritionally, Tom Yam Beef is a moderate-energy, high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with approximately 62 kcal, 5.8 g protein, and 3.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (approximately 420 mg/100 g) derived from fish sauce and broth, which is relevant for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. The low glycemic index (estimated ~45) and low carbohydrate load make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal when prepared with halal-certified beef and fish sauce.

confidence_reason: No exact Tom Yam Beef entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable spiced beef broth soups and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and the food's composition is well-characterised by its known ingredients.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in input JSON was listed as 'Indian', but Tom Yam Beef is a Thai dish. The cuisine has been corrected to 'Thai' based on the dish's established culinary origin. The restaurant 'Baba Eating House' may serve a localised version. 2. Nutrient values are estimated composites; a direct laboratory analysis or HPB Foodo entry would upgrade confidence to high. 3. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for this dish type. 4. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish type. 5. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish type. 6. GI is set to 45 as an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Tom Yam Beef was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. The input cuisine tags included 'Noodles' and 'Drinks & Beverages' which appear to be field survey platform artefacts and are not reflected in the food classification. 8. Halal status is conditional on use of halal-certified beef and fish sauce.
